APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses a clear NLP generation problem—producing constrained, memorable sentence encodings of numbers—using standard linguistic resources (Brown corpus, CMU Pronouncing Dictionary) and language-modeling techniques (n-grams, POS templates) that fit top-tier NLP/ML interests. It includes an appropriate methodological contribution (Sentence Encoder combining POS templates with n-gram scoring) and uses venue-expected evaluation practices, including a human-subject memorability study measuring recall/recognition over time. The topic and audience align well with NLP/ML conferences focused on language generation, usability, and applied evaluation. | B(5): This is squarely within NLP: it focuses on generating natural-language mnemonic encodings using POS templates, n-gram language models, and pronunciation constraints (Brown corpus, CMU Pronouncing Dictionary). The work uses standard NLP methods and evaluation practices for the community (model variants, held-out comparisons, and a human-subject study measuring recall/recognition and time), and it targets an audience interested in language generation and usability. Code and hyperparameters are reported, and the paper is positioned appropriately within NLP research on controllable and memorable text generation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a clear NLP generation problem—producing constrained, memorable sentence encodings of numbers—using standard linguistic resources (Brown corpus, CMU Pronouncing Dictionary) and language-modeling techniques (n-grams, POS templates) that fit top-tier NLP/ML interests. It includes an appropriate methodological contribution (Sentence Encoder combining POS templates with n-gram scoring) and uses venue-expected evaluation practices, including a human-subject memorability study measuring recall/recognition over time. The topic and audience align well with NLP/ML conferences focused on language generation, usability, and applied evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: This is squarely within NLP: it focuses on generating natural-language mnemonic encodings using POS templates, n-gram language models, and pronunciation constraints (Brown corpus, CMU Pronouncing Dictionary). The work uses standard NLP methods and evaluation practices for the community (model variants, held-out comparisons, and a human-subject study measuring recall/recognition and time), and it targets an audience interested in language generation and usability. Code and hyperparameters are reported, and the paper is positioned appropriately within NLP research on controllable and memorable text generation.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses a concrete, useful problem—automatically generating memorable sentence encodings of numbers using the major system—and demonstrates a practical Sentence Encoder (POS templates + n-gram LM) that empirical user testing found more memorable than unigram/random baselines. Code availability and the combination of linguistic templates with language models make the approach reusable and likely to inspire follow-up work in mnemonic generation and usable-security tooling. However, the impact is somewhat limited by the narrow application scope, relatively simple baselines, and the user-study concerns (101 fraudulent responses removed, only 66 valid second-survey responses), so it is more a moderate contribution than a field-changing one. | B(3): The paper addresses a practical problem—automatically producing memorable numeric mnemonics—and demonstrates a concrete improvement: the Sentence Encoder (POS templates + n-gram LM) yielded more memorable password representations than n-gram baselines in a user study. The approach is reproducible (Brown corpus, CMU Pronouncing Dictionary, code and hyperparameters provided) and could be adopted or extended for password memorability and cognitive-assistive applications. However, impact is limited by the study’s modest validated sample (66 second-survey responses after removing 101 fraudulent ones), relatively simple baselines, and some model limitations (e.g., lack of verbs in the POS encoder, slow chunk encoder), so it is useful and likely to inspire follow-up work but not field-changing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a concrete, useful problem—automatically generating memorable sentence encodings of numbers using the major system—and demonstrates a practical Sentence Encoder (POS templates + n-gram LM) that empirical user testing found more memorable than unigram/random baselines. Code availability and the combination of linguistic templates with language models make the approach reusable and likely to inspire follow-up work in mnemonic generation and usable-security tooling. However, the impact is somewhat limited by the narrow application scope, relatively simple baselines, and the user-study concerns (101 fraudulent responses removed, only 66 valid second-survey responses), so it is more a moderate contribution than a field-changing one.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses a practical problem—automatically producing memorable numeric mnemonics—and demonstrates a concrete improvement: the Sentence Encoder (POS templates + n-gram LM) yielded more memorable password representations than n-gram baselines in a user study. The approach is reproducible (Brown corpus, CMU Pronouncing Dictionary, code and hyperparameters provided) and could be adopted or extended for password memorability and cognitive-assistive applications. However, impact is limited by the study’s modest validated sample (66 second-survey responses after removing 101 fraudulent ones), relatively simple baselines, and some model limitations (e.g., lack of verbs in the POS encoder, slow chunk encoder), so it is useful and likely to inspire follow-up work but not field-changing.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies established components (major system, n-gram language models, POS templates, Brown corpus, CMU Pronouncing Dictionary) in a novel assembled system to generate memorable sentence encodings and validates this with a user study showing the Sentence Encoder outperforms simple n-gram baselines. It clearly differentiates its contribution from prior online major-system tools (which produce short single-word encodings) and describes several encoding variants. However, the core techniques are combinations and adaptations of existing methods rather than fundamentally new algorithms or theoretical advances, making the contribution incrementally novel rather than highly original. | B(3): The paper applies known components (the major system, n-gram language models, POS templates, Brown corpus, CMU Pronouncing Dictionary) but combines them into a novel pipeline (the Sentence Encoder) to produce sentence-length mnemonic encodings and evaluates memorability with a user study. This addresses a clearly defined shortcoming of prior online tools (which produce single-word encodings and do not rank outputs), so the application and system design are reasonably new. However, the core techniques are largely an applied integration of existing methods rather than fundamentally new algorithms or theory, so the contribution is moderate rather than highly novel.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies established components (major system, n-gram language models, POS templates, Brown corpus, CMU Pronouncing Dictionary) in a novel assembled system to generate memorable sentence encodings and validates this with a user study showing the Sentence Encoder outperforms simple n-gram baselines. It clearly differentiates its contribution from prior online major-system tools (which produce short single-word encodings) and describes several encoding variants. However, the core techniques are combinations and adaptations of existing methods rather than fundamentally new algorithms or theoretical advances, making the contribution incrementally novel rather than highly original.

This addresses a clearly defined shortcoming of prior online tools (which produce single-word encodings and do not rank outputs), so the application and system design are reasonably new. SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported: multiple encoder variants (trained on the Brown corpus with CMU Pronouncing Dictionary checks) are compared to reasonable baselines, and a user study shows the Sentence Encoder outperforms n-gram baselines on recall and recognition metrics. There are no mathematical derivations to check. The experimental methodology is largely sound (proper baselines, multiple memorability metrics), but the summary omits mention of statistical tests and the human study was reduced to 66 valid second-survey responses after removing 101 fraudulent responses, which raises concerns about power and robustness. Overall the conclusions are plausible given the presented evidence, though the limited cleaned sample size and some reported practical issues (e.g., slow chunk encoder, POS model verb distribution) are minor weaknesses. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments: they train n-gram/POS models on the Brown corpus, use the CMU Pronouncing Dictionary, implement multiple encoders (including the Sentence Encoder) and evaluate memorability via immediate and 7-day recall, recognition, timing, and subjective rankings. Methodological details (hyperparameters, datasets, baselines: Random and Unigram) are reported, but there are minor concerns: the human study had 101 fraudulent responses removed leaving only 66 valid follow-ups, and the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or stronger baseline comparisons. No mathematical derivations are required beyond the described modelling choices. Overall the evidence supports the claims with some limitations in experimental robustness and generalizability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported: multiple encoder variants (trained on the Brown corpus with CMU Pronouncing Dictionary checks) are compared to reasonable baselines, and a user study shows the Sentence Encoder outperforms n-gram baselines on recall and recognition metrics. There are no mathematical derivations to check. The experimental methodology is largely sound (proper baselines, multiple memorability metrics), but the summary omits mention of statistical tests and the human study was reduced to 66 valid second-survey responses after removing 101 fraudulent responses, which raises concerns about power and robustness. Overall the conclusions are plausible given the presented evidence, though the limited cleaned sample size and some reported practical issues (e.g., slow chunk encoder, POS model verb distribution) are minor weaknesses.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments: they train n-gram/POS models on the Brown corpus, use the CMU Pronouncing Dictionary, implement multiple encoders (including the Sentence Encoder) and evaluate memorability via immediate and 7-day recall, recognition, timing, and subjective rankings. Methodological details (hyperparameters, datasets, baselines: Random and Unigram) are reported, but there are minor concerns: the human study had 101 fraudulent responses removed leaving only 66 valid follow-ups, and the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or stronger baseline comparisons. No mathematical derivations are required beyond the described modelling choices. Overall the evidence supports the claims with some limitations in experimental robustness and generalizability.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a solid experimental evaluation: multiple encoder variants (including baselines and preliminary models) are compared, and a user study measures immediate and 7-day recall, recognition, task time, and subjective rankings. Reproducibility is supported by available code and reported hyperparameters, and related work is described adequately. However, there is no explicit ablation or sensitivity analysis isolating the contributions of components (e.g., POS templates vs. n-gram weighting and post-processing), and the user study lost 101 fraudulent responses leaving only 66 valid second-survey participants, which may reduce statistical power. Some failure modes and limitations are discussed (POS encoder verb paucity, chunk encoder slowness), but finer-grained component analyses are missing. | B(4): The paper reports a user study measuring immediate and 7-day recall, recognition, task time, and subjective rankings, compares six encoder variants (including two baselines and three preliminary models plus the final Sentence Encoder), and provides code and hyperparameters for reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as adequate and several failure modes and limitations (e.g., lack of verbs in the POS encoder, slow Chunk encoder parsing, and removal of fraudulent survey responses) are discussed. However, the summary does not indicate systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating the contribution of individual components (e.g., POS templates vs. n-gram rescoring), which is a notable gap. Overall the experiments are solid and convincing but miss some deeper component-wise analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a solid experimental evaluation: multiple encoder variants (including baselines and preliminary models) are compared, and a user study measures immediate and 7-day recall, recognition, task time, and subjective rankings. However, the summary does not indicate systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating the contribution of individual components (e.g., POS templates vs. n-gram rescoring), which is a notable gap. Overall the experiments are solid and convincing but miss some deeper component-wise analysis.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and the methodological flow is clear (intro -> methods describing six encoders using the Brown corpus and CMU Pronouncing Dictionary -> experiments including immediate and 7-day recall, recognition, timing, and subjective rankings -> conclusion). The main contributions are stated early and supported by concrete methodological details (POS + n-gram Sentence Encoder, baselines, hyperparameters, and code availability), and related work coverage is adequate. No major notation or phrasing problems are reported in the summary and writing quality is described as good, though a few presentation details (e.g., limitations and the removal of fraudulent survey responses) are noted rather than deeply discussed, suggesting only minor presentation issues. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from introduction through methods (six encoders described) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (automatic memorable encodings, several encoding models, final Sentence Encoder). Notation and experimental details appear consistently reported (datasets, CMU dictionary, hyperparameters, code availability) and limitations are explicitly discussed (POS encoder verb scarcity, chunk encoder runtime, filtering of fraudulent study responses). Writing quality is good overall, so only minor presentation issues are likely (e.g., some model-specific artifacts and runtime concerns might benefit from clearer exposition).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and the methodological flow is clear (intro -> methods describing six encoders using the Brown corpus and CMU Pronouncing Dictionary -> experiments including immediate and 7-day recall, recognition, timing, and subjective rankings -> conclusion). The main contributions are stated early and supported by concrete methodological details (POS + n-gram Sentence Encoder, baselines, hyperparameters, and code availability), and related work coverage is adequate. MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares its Sentence Encoder against clear baselines (Random and Unigram) and evaluates all methods using the same data sources (Brown corpus, CMU dictionary), the same human-subject metrics (recall, recognition, time, subjective rankings), and reports limitations and failure modes (e.g., POS encoder lacking verbs, chunk encoder runtime). Differences between the proposed method and baselines are described (use of POS templates + n-gram rescoring vs. baseline n-gram/unigram approaches), and related work coverage is noted as adequate. However, the compared baselines are relatively simple and the paper does not appear to include more recent or stronger automated mnemonic or neural baselines, a minor omission. | B(4): The paper evaluates its Sentence Encoder against two baselines (Random and Unigram) using the same datasets and human-subject metrics (recall, recognition, time), and reports detailed analyses of encoder differences and limitations (e.g., POS encoder verb paucity, chunk encoder runtime). Related work coverage is described as adequate and they report when components underperform, and the human study appears to use fair, consistent conditions. However, the baselines are fairly weak and the paper does not compare against existing online major-system tools or stronger modern baselines, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its Sentence Encoder against clear baselines (Random and Unigram) and evaluates all methods using the same data sources (Brown corpus, CMU dictionary), the same human-subject metrics (recall, recognition, time, subjective rankings), and reports limitations and failure modes (e.g., POS encoder lacking verbs, chunk encoder runtime). Differences between the proposed method and baselines are described (use of POS templates + n-gram rescoring vs. baseline n-gram/unigram approaches), and related work coverage is noted as adequate. However, the compared baselines are relatively simple and the paper does not appear to include more recent or stronger automated mnemonic or neural baselines, a minor omission.

Related work coverage is described as adequate and they report when components underperform, and the human study appears to use fair, consistent conditions. main_claims: We present a method to automatically generate memorable sentence encodings of numeric sequences using the major system.

We propose several encoding models and introduce a final Sentence Encoder that combines part-of-speech sentence templates with an n-gram language model to produce syntactically plausible, memorable mnemonics.
A user study shows that the model combining POS templates and an n-gram language model (the Sentence Encoder) produces more memorable password representations than baseline n-gram-based encodings.
_summary.methodology: Train n-gram and POS models on the Brown corpus and use the CMU Pronouncing Dictionary to ensure pronounceability; implement six encoders (two greedy baselines, three preliminary models, and a final Sentence Encoder) where the Sentence Encoder samples POS sentence templates and selects words via trigram scores weighted by digits encoded, with post-processing via bigram rescoring.
limitations: Existing online major-system tools often return many single-word encodings, do not rank encodings, and typically produce only one- or two-word encodings limiting length.
The POS encoder produced a relative lack of verbs in generated sentences.
The chunk encoder had a slow running time due to expensive parsing of noun and verb phrases.
The human-subject study contained 101 fraudulent responses that had to be removed, leaving 66 valid second-survey responses.
_summary.topic_area: NLP / mnemonic generation and password memorability
